rationale.AWR: After well-publicized graduate-student suicides, MIT built MindHandHeart and disaggregated wellbeing surveys with regular review; proactive but not independently audited. Key signal: structured wellbeing-survey program post-crisis.
rationale.EMP: MindHandHeart and a long-standing Ombuds Office exist, but the Aaron Swartz episode and later cases show empathy is process-based rather than reliably felt across populations. Key signal: independent ombuds + mixed survivor testimony.
rationale.ACT: MIT Medical mental-health has invested in same-day urgent access and grew counseling staff, though CCMH-type wait-time pressure persists at peak. Key signal: expanded urgent-care access model.
rationale.EQU: Need-blind admission with no-loan, full-need aid is genuinely strong for a private; high Pell share for an elite and strong completion. Key signal: full-need no-loan aid + need-blind.
rationale.BND: Graduate stipends raised and a recognized grad union (GSU-UE) contract in force, but high-cost Cambridge housing keeps rent burden a live issue. Key signal: ratified grad-worker contract.
rationale.ACC: Title IX office and Clery reporting function; cooperated with the Epstein-donations external review and published findings, but the donation scandal itself was a serious lapse. Key signal: published Goodwin Procter Epstein report.
rationale.SYS: Sustained, multi-year structural investment in student mental health and public advocacy on research/immigration policy; theory-of-change orientation. Key signal: 5+ year wellbeing investment trend.
rationale.INT: Accepted Epstein gifts contrary to stated values; endowment fossil-fuel engagement-not-divestment stance contested by students. Values-behavior gap acknowledged only under pressure. Key signal: Epstein-gift values contradiction.

rationale.AWR: CAPS and the Cardinal at Ease/early-alert programs plus regular wellbeing surveys give multiple detection channels, post a cluster of student deaths that drove reform. Key signal: post-crisis early-alert + survey program.
rationale.EMP: Established Ombuds and survivor-support infrastructure, but historic Title IX handling and student testimony show empathy is uneven. Key signal: independent ombuds, mixed survivor experience.
rationale.ACT: CAPS expanded counselor headcount and same-day access, yet demand growth keeps non-urgent waits high. Key signal: counselor expansion against persistent waitlist.
rationale.EQU: Need-blind, full-need, no-loan aid (free tuition under income thresholds) and meaningful Pell share for a hyper-elite. Key signal: free-tuition income thresholds.
rationale.BND: Recognized SGWU-UE graduate union with a first contract; high Bay Area housing cost keeps grad/postdoc precarity salient. Key signal: ratified grad-union contract.
rationale.ACC: Functioning Title IX/Clery apparatus; the 2023 president's resignation over research-integrity handling shows correction happened but only after external investigative pressure. Key signal: presidential resignation after research-integrity review.
rationale.SYS: Multi-year mental-health investment and significant public-policy engagement; root-cause orientation present but tied to institutional interest. Key signal: sustained wellbeing investment.
rationale.INT: President's mishandling of research-integrity concerns, plus endowment/values tensions, indicate integrity gaps surfaced externally rather than self-corrected. Key signal: research-integrity leadership failure.

rationale.AWR: CAMHS plus repeated student-led mental-health task forces and surveys; detection is structured but reforms were largely student-pressure-driven. Key signal: recurring task-force-driven wellbeing review.
rationale.EMP: Harvard Law was OCR-found in violation on sexual-harassment response; long investigation timelines and the Comaroff harassment case show validation lags. Key signal: OCR finding + slow harm acknowledgment.
rationale.ACT: CAMHS expanded teletherapy/urgent access and added counselors, but historically long non-urgent waits and referral 'therapy by hand-off' criticized. Key signal: expanded access amid waitlist criticism.
rationale.EQU: Generous zero-cost aid below income thresholds and the legacy-admissions debate (post-SFFA) put equity under scrutiny; strong financial access, contested fairness. Key signal: no-cost aid thresholds, legacy scrutiny.
rationale.BND: HGSU-UAW contract secured real protections including grievance routes for harassment; rich resources but high cost of living. Key signal: HGSU-UAW harassment grievance procedure.
rationale.ACC: Antisemitism/2024 congressional turmoil, presidential resignation, and litigation show accountability often reactive and externally forced. Key signal: 2024 leadership crisis handled reactively.
rationale.SYS: Substantial endowment-backed capacity and some policy advocacy, but structural critique constrained by donor and political interests. Key signal: capacity present, advocacy constrained.
rationale.INT: Epstein/donor controversies, legacy admissions, and crisis governance reveal a values-behavior gap acknowledged mainly under external pressure. Key signal: donor-values contradictions.

rationale.AWR: University Counselling Service plus UK University Mental Health Charter (OfS-funded) participation and college welfare tutors give layered, reviewed detection. Key signal: UMHC participation + collegiate welfare system.
rationale.EMP: College tutorial system gives close pastoral contact, but a confederal structure means experience varies sharply by college; no single independent ombuds. Key signal: variable collegiate pastoral care.
rationale.ACT: Counselling Service with defined access standards and college-level support, though demand outpaces capacity at peaks. Key signal: defined counselling access standards.
rationale.EQU: Access and Participation Plan with OfS targets, UNIQ outreach, and Race Equality Charter Bronze (2024); persistent state-school/POLAR gaps remain. Key signal: OfS-bound APP with measured targets.
rationale.BND: Oxford UCU documents an entrenched casualisation crisis; 500+ signed an open letter in 2024 demanding reform. Key signal: documented casualisation crisis (UCU report).
rationale.ACC: Subject to OfS regulation and public APP reporting; handles complaints through OIA route, providing external adjudication. Key signal: OIA + OfS external accountability.
rationale.SYS: Long-horizon access strategy (Graduate Access Strategy 2024-29) and sector advocacy show genuine root-cause work. Key signal: multi-year access strategy.
rationale.INT: Public APP commitments are largely honored and regulator-enforced, but casualisation persistence shows a values-behavior gap on staff treatment. Key signal: regulated commitments vs. staff precarity.

rationale.AWR: University Counselling Service, college welfare teams, and UMHC participation give layered detection; reviewed but collegiate-variable. Key signal: UMHC + collegiate welfare.
rationale.EMP: Close tutorial pastoral contact, but experience varies by college and there is no single independent ombuds; empathy is structurally uneven. Key signal: variable collegiate pastoral care.
rationale.ACT: Counselling Service with access standards and a Disability Resource Centre; capacity strained at peak demand. Key signal: defined access standards.
rationale.EQU: OfS Access and Participation Plan, Foundation Year for under-represented students, and the Oxbridge 'Close the Gap' work; state-school gaps persist. Key signal: APP + Foundation Year.
rationale.BND: Cambridge UCU has actively balloted on casualisation, evidencing precarious contingent-staff conditions. Key signal: UCU casualisation ballot.
rationale.ACC: OfS-regulated with OIA external complaints route and public reporting. Key signal: external regulatory accountability.
rationale.SYS: Multi-year access strategy and sector advocacy indicate root-cause orientation. Key signal: long-horizon access planning.
rationale.INT: Regulator-enforced commitments largely honored; casualisation persistence is the main values gap. Key signal: commitments vs. staff precarity.

rationale.AWR: Caltech Counseling Center and wellness programming serve a tiny, intense student body; detection exists but the high-pressure culture is a known stressor with limited disaggregated review. Key signal: counseling for high-pressure small cohort.
rationale.EMP: Small-scale advising offers closeness, but reports of an intense, sometimes punishing research culture and limited independent ombuds presence temper this. Key signal: intense culture, limited independent advocacy.
rationale.ACT: Counseling Center with reasonable access given small enrollment; resources concentrated but demand for STEM-stress support is high. Key signal: small-cohort access advantage.
rationale.EQU: Need-blind, full-need aid and a strong STEM-equity push, though Pell share and demographic diversity historically lag peers. Key signal: full-need aid, diversity lag.
rationale.BND: No grad union recognized for years; renamed buildings after eugenics review shows some boundary-setting, but grad-labor protections thinner than unionized peers. Key signal: limited grad-labor protection.
rationale.ACC: Conducted a public review and renamed buildings tied to eugenicist legacy — a real accountability act — but routine Title IX transparency is modest. Key signal: eugenics-legacy review and renaming.
rationale.SYS: Strong research mission but limited evidence of structural higher-ed-access advocacy at institutional cost. Key signal: thin structural-advocacy evidence.
rationale.INT: Eugenics-legacy renaming was a genuine non-performative correction; endowment-values disclosure otherwise limited. Key signal: legacy renaming as values act.

rationale.AWR: UHS Counseling, embedded 'Counselors in Academic Residence,' and basic-needs centers provide multiple proactive detection channels with disaggregated equity focus. Key signal: embedded counselors + basic-needs detection.
rationale.EMP: Strong basic-needs and undocumented-student support culture, but the scale of a huge public means many feel processed; survivor-support reforms followed prior Title IX failures. Key signal: basic-needs culture vs. scale.
rationale.ACT: Emergency aid, food pantry, and basic-needs center deliver timely material help; counseling waits remain a strain at scale. Key signal: operational basic-needs/emergency aid.
rationale.EQU: Among the strongest elite-tier Pell access in the world (large Pell share, strong transfer/first-gen pipelines) plus undocumented-student legal services. Key signal: high Pell share + first-gen pipeline.
rationale.BND: 2022 UAW strike exposed grad workers paying >50% of income on rent; subsequent raises helped but housing precarity persists. Key signal: 2022 UAW rent-burden strike.
rationale.ACC: History of OCR Title IX cases and faculty-misconduct scandals; corrections came largely under external/state pressure. Key signal: OCR history, externally driven correction.
rationale.SYS: Genuine structural advocacy on access, undocumented students, and public-funding defense at political cost. Key signal: structural access advocacy.
rationale.INT: UC system fossil-fuel divestment (completed) and sustained public-mission commitments show values backed by costly action. Key signal: completed UC fossil-fuel divestment.

rationale.AWR: CPS plus a notable post-criticism reform of its leave-of-absence/readmission policy shows it acted on detected mental-health harm. Key signal: leave-policy reform after student advocacy.
rationale.EMP: Reformed involuntary-leave practices and improved readmission empathy, though earlier policies pressured students out; ombuds-type advocacy improving. Key signal: empathy-driven leave-policy change.
rationale.ACT: Well-resourced CPS with strong counselor ratios for its small size and robust emergency aid via its large per-student endowment. Key signal: favorable counselor ratio + emergency aid.
rationale.EQU: Pioneered no-loan aid (2001), free tuition under income thresholds, and rising Pell share; among the most generous access models. Key signal: founding no-loan, free-tuition thresholds.
rationale.BND: PGSU organizing ongoing; strong stipends and guaranteed multi-year funding reduce grad precarity relative to peers. Key signal: guaranteed multi-year grad funding.
rationale.ACC: Functioning Title IX/Clery; reformed mental-health leave policy after external criticism — correction shown but pressure-prompted. Key signal: policy correction post-criticism.
rationale.SYS: Sustained access investment and public service mission ('in the nation's service'); some structural advocacy. Key signal: long-running access investment.
rationale.INT: Generous aid maintained without reputational necessity and leave-policy reform suggest values backed by action; endowment-values debates remain. Key signal: sustained costly aid commitment.

rationale.AWR: Student counselling and UMHC participation exist, but an independent report exposed an 'endemic culture of bullying' that detection systems failed to surface for staff. Key signal: bullying report revealing detection failure.
rationale.EMP: Independent inquiry found senior leaders bullied staff and contributed to a bullying culture hitting marginalized staff hardest — a serious empathy failure at the top. Key signal: leadership-bullying findings.
rationale.ACT: Counselling and wellbeing services function for students, but staff harm response was slow and contested. Key signal: student services vs. weak staff-harm response.
rationale.EQU: Outreach and OfS APP commitments exist; the bullying report explicitly flagged racism and misogyny, undercutting equity claims. Key signal: report-documented racism/misogyny.
rationale.BND: UCU documents bullying, casualisation, and ongoing pay/pension disputes with strike action — poor labor-condition signals. Key signal: UCU strikes + bullying culture.
rationale.ACC: Leadership initially admitted bullying but resisted resignation calls; accountability was partial and externally forced. Key signal: contested, partial accountability.
rationale.SYS: STEM-mission strength and some sector engagement, but structural self-critique limited given internal culture findings. Key signal: limited internal structural critique.
rationale.INT: Senior executives admitting bullying while remaining in post is a stark values-behavior gap. Key signal: leadership bullying admission without full remedy.

rationale.AWR: Student Wellness counseling and an early-alert apparatus exist, but a notoriously intense academic culture and South Side safety stressors keep distress high; detection reviewed but not audited. Key signal: counseling amid high-pressure culture.
rationale.EMP: Kalven-report free-expression commitment is principled, but students report a transactional, rigorous culture; ombuds-style advocacy limited. Key signal: principled but transactional climate.
rationale.ACT: Counseling expanded sessions and added staff after student pressure over session caps; emergency aid exists but historically capped. Key signal: session-cap reform after advocacy.
rationale.EQU: No-Barriers/UChicago Promise (free tuition under income thresholds) and strong neighborhood-access programs; Pell share improving. Key signal: No-Barriers free-tuition thresholds.
rationale.BND: Graduate Students United gained recognition and a first contract after a long, contested fight the university initially resisted. Key signal: GSU first contract after resistance.
rationale.ACC: Functioning Title IX/Clery; the university's private police force and community-relations record draw sustained criticism, addressed reactively. Key signal: private-police accountability scrutiny.
rationale.SYS: Some community-investment and policy work on the South Side, but structural critique constrained by institutional interest. Key signal: South Side community investment.
rationale.INT: Free-expression principles upheld at reputational cost (a genuine integrity signal), but grad-union resistance and endowment-values debates cut the other way. Key signal: principled free-expression stance.

rationale.AWR: Psychological Counselling Services and a Respect campaign give detection channels; ETH responded to a high-profile professorial-bullying case by building survey/monitoring tools. Key signal: post-bullying-scandal monitoring tools.
rationale.EMP: The 2018 doctoral-student bullying scandal exposed empathy failures, but ETH created an Ombuds and Respect office in response, improving validation pathways. Key signal: ombuds created after bullying case.
rationale.ACT: Counselling, low Swiss public tuition, and doctoral support structures give reasonable, timely response. Key signal: accessible counselling + low-fee structure.
rationale.EQU: Very low tuition aids broad access, though gender balance in STEM and international-student support remain works in progress; disclosure partly German. Key signal: low-fee access, gender-balance gap.
rationale.BND: Doctoral researchers are paid employees with strong Swiss labor protections and benefits — structurally better than US grad-student precarity. Key signal: employee-status doctoral researchers.
rationale.ACC: ETH publicly investigated and acted on the professorial-bullying case, restructuring doctoral oversight — a real, somewhat self-initiated correction. Key signal: structural reform after misconduct finding.
rationale.SYS: Strong long-horizon planning and public-research mission; some policy engagement, partly in national language. Key signal: long-horizon institutional planning.
rationale.INT: Acting decisively on the bullying scandal and reforming governance signals values backed by structural change. Key signal: governance reform as values act.

rationale.AWR: Yale Mental Health & Counseling is large, but the 2022 lawsuit showed it detected disability disclosures and responded by pushing students out — detection that harmed. Key signal: disability-discrimination lawsuit over leave practices.
rationale.EMP: The 'Elis for Rachel' suit alleged Yale pressured students with mental-health disabilities to withdraw without deference to treating clinicians — a major empathy/validation failure. Key signal: documented withdrawal-pressure practice.
rationale.ACT: Large counseling operation that expanded telehealth and access; historically long waits and the leave-policy harms tempered efficacy. Key signal: scale vs. access-quality concerns.
rationale.EQU: Need-blind, full-need, zero-parent-contribution under income thresholds, with strong Pell trajectory. Key signal: zero-contribution aid thresholds.
rationale.BND: Local 33 (UNITE HERE) finally won grad-worker recognition after a decades-long fight Yale resisted; consent/leave issues were the lawsuit's core. Key signal: long-resisted grad-union recognition.
rationale.ACC: Yale settled the 2023 disability suit and substantively reformed leave-of-absence and reinstatement policy — a concrete, externally-prompted reparative correction. Key signal: 2023 settlement + policy reform.
rationale.SYS: Sustained mental-health investment (partly prompted by litigation) and some structural advocacy. Key signal: litigation-driven systemic reform.
rationale.INT: Reformed only after a lawsuit and decades of grad-union resistance indicate values lagged behavior until external pressure. Key signal: reform contingent on legal pressure.

rationale.AWR: After a cluster of student suicides, Penn created a Task Force on Student Psychological Health and 'Penn Wellise'/CAPS reforms with ongoing review. Key signal: post-suicide-cluster task force.
rationale.EMP: Reforms followed tragedy and student pressure; CAPS access improved but a high-pressure 'Penn Face' culture and uneven validation persist. Key signal: culture-driven empathy strain.
rationale.ACT: CAPS expanded counselors and same-day/urgent access; emergency aid exists. Demand keeps non-urgent waits a concern. Key signal: urgent-access expansion.
rationale.EQU: All-grant, no-loan aid and strong Pell trajectory; access generous though legacy/donor influence debated. Key signal: all-grant no-loan aid.
rationale.BND: GET-UP grad organizing ongoing; medical/health-system labor and high-cost-area grad precarity are live issues. Key signal: ongoing grad-labor organizing.
rationale.ACC: Functioning Title IX/Clery, but the 2023-24 antisemitism/congressional crisis and presidential resignation reflect reactive, externally-forced accountability. Key signal: 2023-24 leadership crisis.
rationale.SYS: Sustained wellbeing investment and some civic engagement (e.g., West Philadelphia), but PILOTs/community-equity critiques persist. Key signal: contested community investment.
rationale.INT: Donor-pressure dynamics in the 2023-24 crisis and PILOT disputes reveal a values-behavior gap surfaced under external pressure. Key signal: donor-pressure governance.

rationale.AWR: Student Psychological & Counselling Services plus UMHC participation; UCL ran a notable eugenics-legacy inquiry surfacing institutional harm. Key signal: UMHC + eugenics inquiry.
rationale.EMP: Large, somewhat impersonal London institution, but the eugenics inquiry and de-naming showed willingness to validate historical harm. Key signal: harm-acknowledging eugenics inquiry.
rationale.ACT: Counselling services with defined access and disability support; capacity strained by scale. Key signal: defined counselling access at scale.
rationale.EQU: Strong widening-participation profile for a London Russell Group, OfS APP targets, and access scholarships; founded explicitly non-sectarian. Key signal: strong WP + non-sectarian founding ethos.
rationale.BND: UCU casualisation and pay/pension disputes apply here as sector-wide; contingent-staff precarity is a real issue. Key signal: sector casualisation pressure.
rationale.ACC: The 2018-20 eugenics inquiry led UCL to formally apologize and rename Galton/Pearson facilities — a concrete, comparatively self-initiated reparative act. Key signal: eugenics apology + renaming.
rationale.SYS: Long-horizon access strategy and public-research mission; documented structural reckoning with its own history. Key signal: structural historical reckoning.
rationale.INT: Acting on the eugenics legacy despite reputational discomfort signals values backed by costly action. Key signal: renaming as non-performative correction.

rationale.AWR: After student-death clusters Cornell built a nationally cited public-health/means-restriction mental-health model and Caring Community framework with ongoing review. Key signal: public-health mental-health model post-crisis.
rationale.EMP: The Caring Community approach and CAPS reforms improved validation, though a competitive culture and prior leave-policy criticism temper it. Key signal: Caring Community framework.
rationale.ACT: CAPS expanded access and Cornell's means-restriction safety work is evidence-informed; emergency aid via Access Fund. Key signal: evidence-informed safety intervention.
rationale.EQU: Need-blind, no-loan for lower incomes, and the land-grant CALS/statutory-college mission broadens NY access; strong Pell for the Ivy tier. Key signal: land-grant access + no-loan aid.
rationale.BND: CGSU-UE won recognition and a first contract after a long organizing effort; high-cost Ithaca grad housing remains a strain. Key signal: CGSU-UE first contract.
rationale.ACC: Functioning Title IX/Clery; addressed mental-health crises with documented programmatic change, though some reforms followed loss. Key signal: programmatic change after crises.
rationale.SYS: Land-grant public-engagement mission and sustained mental-health investment give genuine root-cause orientation. Key signal: land-grant systemic mission.
rationale.INT: Sustained safety/wellbeing investment without pure reputational upside is a positive integrity signal; endowment-values debates persist. Key signal: sustained safety investment.

rationale.AWR: Tsinghua operates a well-known psychological counseling center and student case studies document anxiety/depression prevalence, but English-public disaggregated review is thin. Scored conservatively mid-scale per low evidence depth. Key signal: established counseling center, limited public data.
rationale.EMP: Strong faculty-mentorship norms exist, but extreme academic pressure and limited independent ombuds/grievance transparency constrain assessed empathy; evidence largely in Chinese. Key signal: intense pressure, limited grievance transparency.
rationale.ACT: Counseling and academic-support services are resourced for a flagship, but wait-time/outcome data is not publicly verifiable in English. Scored mid-scale. Key signal: resourced services, unverifiable outcomes.
rationale.EQU: National need-based aid and rural-quota access programs broaden entry, but completion-gap and disaggregated-equity data are not openly published. Key signal: national aid + rural quotas, thin disclosure.
rationale.BND: Graduate-researcher labor protections and consent norms are governed centrally with no independent union; high-workload 'neijuan' pressure is documented anecdotally. Scored conservatively. Key signal: no independent labor representation.
rationale.ACC: No queryable independent Title-IX-equivalent or external regulator record is publicly accessible; institutional self-disclosure of harm is limited. Scored low-mid on evidence absence, not proven harm. Key signal: limited external accountability mechanism.
rationale.SYS: Substantial state-backed long-horizon planning and research investment, but structural-critique advocacy independent of the state is not expected or evidenced. Key signal: strong planning, constrained critique.
rationale.INT: Endowment/values transparency and independent integrity audits are not publicly available in English; scored conservatively mid-scale. Key signal: limited public integrity disclosure.

rationale.AWR: PKU has a long-standing mental-health/counseling center and prominent national suicide-prevention research, but disaggregated institutional detection data is not publicly available in English. Scored conservatively. Key signal: counseling center + national MH research.
rationale.EMP: Mentorship norms exist, yet extreme competition ('neijuan') and limited independent grievance transparency constrain assessed empathy; evidence largely Chinese-language. Key signal: high pressure, limited grievance visibility.
rationale.ACT: Flagship-level counseling and academic support are resourced, but wait-time/outcome metrics are not publicly verifiable. Scored mid-scale. Key signal: resourced services, unverifiable outcomes.
rationale.EQU: National need-based aid and rural-access quotas broaden entry; completion-gap and equity disaggregation not openly published. Key signal: national aid + quotas, thin disclosure.
rationale.BND: No independent grad union; centrally governed labor and consent norms with documented high-workload pressure anecdotally. Scored conservatively. Key signal: no independent labor representation.
rationale.ACC: No publicly queryable independent regulator/Title-IX-equivalent record; institutional harm self-disclosure limited. Scored low-mid on evidence absence. Key signal: limited external accountability.
rationale.SYS: Strong state-backed long-horizon planning and research, including national mental-health work, but independent structural critique not expected. Key signal: strong planning, constrained critique.
rationale.INT: Endowment/values and independent integrity-audit transparency not publicly available in English; scored conservatively. Key signal: limited public integrity disclosure.

rationale.AWR: Counseling Center and a major schools-of-public-health mental-health research base inform detection; the private-police plan showed it can misjudge community safety needs. Key signal: strong MH research, contested safety read.
rationale.EMP: TRU-UE won the right to protest 'without being met with force' — language that emerged because students feared the new private police force; empathy gaps drove that demand. Key signal: union-bargained anti-force protection.
rationale.ACT: Well-resourced counseling and a billion-dollar Bloomberg aid gift fund robust financial response; emergency aid available. Key signal: large funded aid + counseling.
rationale.EQU: Bloomberg's $1.8B gift made JHU effectively loan-free and need-blind with rising Pell share — a major access advance. Key signal: $1.8B loan-elimination gift.
rationale.BND: TRU-UE Local 197 first contract (2024) won a $47k+ stipend floor, relocation aid, and protest protections — strong grad-labor gains. Key signal: 2024 grad-union contract terms.
rationale.ACC: The private-police initiative drew sustained community and student opposition; the university advanced it over objections, accountability contested. Key signal: private-police controversy.
rationale.SYS: Public-health mission and large-scale community/health investment in Baltimore give real root-cause orientation, though town-gown tensions persist. Key signal: public-health systemic mission.
rationale.INT: Pressing ahead with private police against community wishes while bargaining away force protections shows a values-behavior tension. Key signal: private-police vs. community values.

rationale.AWR: Counseling and Psychological Services exist, but the 2024-25 protest turmoil and abrupt disciplinary escalation suggest distress detection was overtaken by enforcement. Key signal: enforcement-over-care during protests.
rationale.EMP: Mass suspensions/expulsions of protesters and contested handling of both pro-Palestinian and Jewish students' safety reflect serious empathy and validation failures across groups. Key signal: punitive protest response.
rationale.ACT: CPS and emergency aid function, but institutional bandwidth in 2024-25 shifted heavily to crisis management and federal-funding defense. Key signal: services overshadowed by crisis.
rationale.EQU: Strong need-blind, full-need aid and high Pell share via its access programs remain genuine equity strengths despite the turmoil. Key signal: need-blind full-need aid.
rationale.BND: SWC-UAW grad union has a contract, but the consent/safety environment during encampments and policing was contested; protest discipline strained boundaries. Key signal: grad contract amid contested protest policing.
rationale.ACC: Leadership turnover, congressional pressure, and disputed disciplinary records (incl. a lawsuit over sharing records with Congress) show reactive, externally-driven accountability. Key signal: records-sharing lawsuit + leadership churn.
rationale.SYS: Some structural engagement, but 2024-25 events showed short-term crisis and funding pressures dominating over root-cause work. Key signal: crisis-dominated agenda.
rationale.INT: Capitulation to external political/funding pressure and shifting disciplinary stances indicate commitments bending under pressure. Key signal: commitments bending under federal pressure.

rationale.AWR: After student suicides and a 2019-20 task force, U of T publishes an annual Student Mental Health Report and runs 24/7 multilingual crisis support — strong, reviewed, disaggregated detection. Key signal: annual public MH report + 24/7 crisis line.
rationale.EMP: Reforms followed student deaths and a contested mandated-leave policy that students criticized as punitive; empathy improved but the leave policy episode showed validation gaps. Key signal: mandated-leave-policy controversy + reform.
rationale.ACT: Committed C$3M/year, added 11 counsellors and doubled accessibility advisers, plus hospital partnerships for acute care — concrete, timely scaling to need. Key signal: $3M/yr investment + staffing increases.
rationale.EQU: Broad public-access mission, strong financial-aid guarantees (UTAPS), and large international/first-gen populations; completion-gap disclosure moderate. Key signal: UTAPS aid guarantee.
rationale.BND: CUPE 3902 represents TAs/contract instructors with established contracts; high-cost Toronto housing pressures grad students. Key signal: CUPE 3902 collective agreements.
rationale.ACC: Publishes annual MH outcomes and acted on task-force recommendations; the mandated-leave policy drew human-rights scrutiny addressed partly under pressure. Key signal: public reporting + leave-policy scrutiny.
rationale.SYS: Multi-year mental-health strategy, hospital-system partnerships, and public advocacy for municipal mental-health funding show genuine root-cause and interconnection work. Key signal: city-council MH funding advocacy.
rationale.INT: Sustained, publicly reported investment and continuity across leadership suggest values embedded in policy rather than personality. Key signal: institutionalized, reported MH strategy.
